---
author: Tristan Muno 
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: ../references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    preview-links: false
    slideNumber: true 
    incremental: true
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "View source"
    pdfSeparateFragments: true
    footer:  QMIR -- {{< meta date >}} --  Week 4 
    logo:  ./images/uma_ss.png
    theme: theme.scss
editor: source
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true
  freeze: true
---

## QMIR 2026

### Week 4: The Tidyverse & Tidy Data

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}} <a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a> <br> {{< meta date >}}

# Leftovers & Housekeeping

---

## Git & GitHub -- A Folder Structure Reminder {.smaller}

A few students ran into problems with last week's homework. Here is a summary of how to set things up correctly, once and for all.

. . .

**One dedicated QMIR folder**, at a stable path on your computer:

```
Documents/
  QMIR_2026/          ← your top-level course folder
    week02_exercises/ ← individual repos go INSIDE here
    week03_exercises/
    week04_exercises/
    my_project/
```

. . .

::: {.nonincremental}
- ✅ Place individual repositories **inside** the QMIR folder
- ❌ Do **not** turn the top-level `QMIR_2026/` folder itself into a Git repo — Git repos inside Git repos cause problems
- ❌ Do **not** place your QMIR folder inside a **cloud-synced folder** (iCloud Drive, Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox) — syncing services and Git conflict with each other and corrupt repositories
:::

. . .

> Once this is set up correctly, you should not need to think about it again. Git + GitHub will just work.

---

## Homework Reminder -- `error: true` {.smaller}

If your code has an error and you cannot fix it before the deadline, you can still submit a **fully rendered PDF**:

```yaml
execute:
  error: true
```

Setting `error: true` in the YAML header tells Quarto to **keep rendering even when a code cell fails**. The error message will appear in the output instead of blocking the render.

. . .

This is not an invitation to submit broken code -- it is a safety net. You should always try to fix errors. But if you are stuck, a rendered document with an error message is better than no submission.

---

## Week 4 Goals

By the end of today, you should:

1. Understand what the tidyverse is and why it exists
2. Know what tidy data means and why it matters
3. Be able to import data with `readr`
4. Be able to explore data with `glimpse()`, `head()`, `summary()`
5. Be able to wrangle data with the core `dplyr` verbs
6. Have a first intuition for `ggplot2`
7. Know how to use Quarto cell options for labels, captions, and execution control

---

## Agenda

1. The Tidyverse -- What and Why
2. Tidy Data
3. The Data Science Cycle
4. Data Import with `readr`
5. Data Exploration with `glimpse()`
6. Data Manipulation with `dplyr`
7. A First Look at `ggplot2`
8. Quarto: Cell Options & Cross-References
9. Walkthrough & Exercises

# Part I: The Tidyverse

---

## The Tidyverse -- A Coherent Ecosystem {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

Last week: packages are collections of functions.

The **tidyverse** is a collection of packages that share:

- a common philosophy of how data should be structured
- a consistent grammar (functions behave predictably)
- seamless interoperability -- they are designed to work together

```{r}
#| message: false
library(tidyverse)
```

One `library()` call loads the core set.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

![Source: Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel (2020)](https://education.rstudio.com/blog/2020/07/teaching-the-tidyverse-in-2020-part-1-getting-started/img/tidyverse-packages.png){width="90%"}

:::
::::::

---

## Core Tidyverse Packages {.smaller}

| Package | Role | Key functions |
|---|---|---|
| `readr` | Import rectangular data | `read_csv()`, `read_tsv()` |
| `tibble` | Modern data frames | `tibble()`, `as_tibble()` |
| `dplyr` | Data manipulation | `filter()`, `select()`, `mutate()`, `summarise()`, `group_by()` |
| `tidyr` | Reshape data | `pivot_longer()`, `pivot_wider()` |
| `ggplot2` | Data visualisation | `ggplot()`, `geom_*()` |
| `stringr` | String manipulation | `str_detect()`, `str_replace()` |
| `forcats` | Factor handling | `fct_reorder()`, `fct_relevel()` |
| `purrr` | Functional programming | `map()`, `map_df()` |

. . .

> Today we focus on `readr`, `dplyr`, and `ggplot2`. The others will appear naturally as the course progresses.

---

## The Tibble -- A Better Data Frame {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

The tidyverse uses **tibbles** instead of base R data frames.

They are the same idea -- rows × columns, each column one type -- but with better defaults:

- Prints only the first 10 rows
- Shows column types below the header
- Never converts strings to factors silently
- Never creates row names

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| message: false
library(gapminder)

# gapminder is already a tibble
gapminder
```

:::
::::::

# Part II: Tidy Data

---

## What is Tidy Data?

> *Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way.*
>
> -- Hadley Wickham

. . .

A dataset is **tidy** when:

1. Each **variable** has its own **column**
2. Each **observation** has its own **row**
3. Each **value** has its own **cell**

---

## Tidy vs. Wide Data -- An Example {.smaller}

**Wide format** (common in spreadsheets, not tidy):

| country | gdpPercap_1952 | gdpPercap_1957 | gdpPercap_1962 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Germany | 7144 | 10188 | 12902 |
| France | 7029 | 8663 | 10560 |

. . .

**Long (tidy) format** (what the tidyverse expects):

| country | year | gdpPercap |
|---|---|---|
| Germany | 1952 | 7144 |
| Germany | 1957 | 10188 |
| France | 1952 | 7029 |
| France | 1957 | 8663 |

. . .

> In the tidy version, `year` is a variable -- a column. In the wide version, year is hiding inside column names. The tidyverse (and especially `ggplot2`) expects tidy data.

---

## Reshaping with `tidyr` -- A Brief Preview {.smaller}

You will encounter wide data in the wild. `tidyr` provides two functions to convert between formats:

- `pivot_longer()` -- wide → long (tidy)
- `pivot_wider()` -- long → wide

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Hypothetical wide dataset
wide_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("gdpPercap_"),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdpPercap"
  )
```

. . .

We won't go deep on reshaping today -- the gapminder data is already tidy. But knowing these functions exist will save you hours when you start working with real-world data.

# Part III: The Data Science Cycle

---

## The Data Science Cycle {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

Everything we do in this course fits somewhere in this cycle:

**Import** → **Tidy** → **Transform** → **Visualise** → **Model** → **Communicate**

. . .

Where we are today:

- ✅ **Import** -- `readr`
- ✅ **Tidy** -- we got lucky, gapminder is already tidy
- ✅ **Transform** -- `dplyr`
- 👀 **Visualise** -- `ggplot2` preview
- 📅 **Model** -- coming later in the course
- ✅ **Communicate** -- Quarto, all along

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

![Source: R for Data Science (Wickham et al.)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/diagrams/data-science/base.png){width="100%"}

:::
::::::

# Part IV: Data Import

---

## Installing and Loading Packages {.smaller}

Before you can use a package, you need to:

1. **Install** it once (downloads from CRAN to your computer)
2. **Load** it in each session (makes its functions available)

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Install once -- you only need to do this one time
install.packages("gapminder")
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load every session -- goes at the top of your .qmd
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
Never put `install.packages()` inside a Quarto document you intend to render or share. It will re-install the package every time the document is rendered and may cause errors in others' environments.
:::

---

## Data Import with `readr` {.smaller}

`readr` is the tidyverse package for importing rectangular data files.

| Function | File type |
|---|---|
| `read_csv("file.csv")` | Comma-separated values |
| `read_tsv("file.tsv")` | Tab-separated values |
| `read_delim("file.txt", delim = ";")` | Any delimiter |

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Import a CSV file and assign it to an object
my_data <- read_csv("data/my_dataset.csv")
```

. . .

For other formats:

```{r}
#| eval: false

library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/dataset.dta") # Stata
my_data <- read_sav("data/dataset.sav") # SPSS

library(readxl)
my_data <- read_excel("data/dataset.xlsx") # Excel
```

. . .

> **Always assign the result to an object.** `read_csv("file.csv")` alone reads the file but immediately discards it.

---

## Data Export {.smaller}

To save a data frame to disk:

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Write to CSV
write_csv(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.csv")

# Write to RDS (R's native format -- preserves types exactly)
saveRDS(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.rds")

# Read it back
my_data <- readRDS("output/my_dataset_clean.rds")
```

. . .

| Format | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| `.csv` | Universal, opens in Excel | Loses type info (factors become strings) |
| `.rds` | Preserves all R types exactly | Only readable in R |
| `.dta` | Stata-compatible | Requires `haven` |

# Part V: Data Exploration

---

## Your First Questions When You See a Dataset {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Compact overview: types and first values
glimpse(gapminder)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Summary statistics per column
summary(gapminder)
```

:::
::::::

---

## More Exploration Functions {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# First n rows
head(gapminder, 5)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Dimensions and column names
nrow(gapminder)
ncol(gapminder)
names(gapminder)
```

:::
::::::

---

## Frequency Tables with `table()` {.smaller}

```{r}
# How many observations per continent?
table(gapminder$continent)
```

. . .

```{r}
# How many unique years are in the data?
length(unique(gapminder$year))
unique(gapminder$year)
```

# Part VI: Data Manipulation with `dplyr`

---

## `dplyr` -- A Grammar of Data Manipulation {.smaller}

All `dplyr` functions follow the same pattern:

- **First argument**: the data frame
- **Subsequent arguments**: what to do
- **Return value**: always a (new) data frame

. . .

The core verbs:

| Verb | What it does |
|---|---|
| `filter()` | Keep rows that meet a condition |
| `select()` | Keep or drop columns |
| `mutate()` | Create or modify variables |
| `arrange()` | Sort rows |
| `summarise()` | Compute summary statistics |
| `group_by()` | Group rows for grouped operations |
| `ungroup()` | Remove grouping |

---

## `filter()` -- Keep Rows by Condition {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Keep only European countries
gapminder |>
  filter(continent == "Europe")
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Multiple conditions -- AND
gapminder |>
  filter(
    continent == "Europe" &
      year == 2007
  )
```

:::
::::::

---

## `filter()` -- More Examples {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# OR condition
gapminder |>
  filter(
    country == "Germany" |
      country == "France"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# %in% for multiple values
gapminder |>
  filter(
    country %in% c("Germany", "France", "Italy")
  )
```

:::
::::::

---

## `select()` -- Keep or Drop Columns {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Keep specific columns
gapminder |>
  select(country, year, lifeExp)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Drop columns with minus
gapminder |>
  select(-continent, -pop)
```

:::
::::::

---

## `mutate()` -- Create or Modify Variables {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Create a new variable: total GDP
gapminder |>
  mutate(gdp_total = pop * gdpPercap) |>
  select(country, year, gdp_total)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Modify an existing variable: GDP in billions
gapminder |>
  mutate(
    gdp_billions = (pop * gdpPercap) / 1e9
  ) |>
  select(country, year, gdp_billions)
```

:::
::::::

---

## `arrange()` -- Sort Rows {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Ascending (default)
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  arrange(lifeExp) |>
  select(country, lifeExp)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Descending with desc()
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  arrange(desc(lifeExp)) |>
  select(country, lifeExp)
```

:::
::::::

---

## `summarise()` -- Compute Summary Statistics {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Overall mean life expectancy
gapminder |>
  summarise(
    mean_lifeExp = mean(lifeExp),
    median_lifeExp = median(lifeExp),
    n = n()
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Multiple summary statistics at once
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  summarise(
    mean_gdp = mean(gdpPercap),
    max_gdp = max(gdpPercap),
    min_gdp = min(gdpPercap)
  )
```

:::
::::::

---

## `group_by()` + `summarise()` -- The Power Combo {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Mean life expectancy by continent
gapminder |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(mean_lifeExp = mean(lifeExp))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
# Mean life expectancy by continent and year
gapminder |>
  group_by(continent, year) |>
  summarise(
    mean_lifeExp = mean(lifeExp),
    .groups = "drop" # good practice: always ungroup after
  )
```

:::
::::::

---

## Putting It Together -- A Full Pipeline {.smaller}

```{r}
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  mutate(gdp_billions = (pop * gdpPercap) / 1e9) |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(
    mean_lifeExp = round(mean(lifeExp), 1),
    total_gdp_bn = round(sum(gdp_billions), 0),
    n_countries = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_lifeExp))
```

. . .

> Read this as: *take gapminder, then keep only 2007, then compute GDP in billions, then group by continent, then summarise, then sort by life expectancy descending.*

---

## Bad Practice vs. Good Practice {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Bad practice** -- nested or intermediate objects:

```{r}
#| eval: false

gap_2007 <- filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
gap_gdp <- mutate(gap_2007, gdp = pop * gdpPercap)
gap_grp <- group_by(gap_gdp, continent)
summarise(gap_grp, mean_gdp = mean(gdp))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Good practice** -- piped chain:

```{r}
#| eval: false

gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  mutate(gdp = pop * gdpPercap) |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(mean_gdp = mean(gdp))
```

:::
::::::

. . .

Both produce the same result. The piped version reads like a sentence and requires no intermediate objects.

# Part VII: A First Look at `ggplot2`

---

## The Grammar of Graphics {.smaller}

`ggplot2` builds plots from **layers**. Every plot starts the same way:

```r
ggplot(data = <DATA>, aes(x = <VAR>, y = <VAR>, ...)) +
  geom_*()
```

. . .

The three essential pieces:

- **`ggplot()`** -- sets up the canvas and declares the data
- **`aes()`** -- maps variables to visual properties (x, y, colour, size, ...)
- **`geom_*()`** -- the geometric object that draws the data (points, lines, bars, ...)

. . .

> Note the `+` operator, not the pipe `|>`. ggplot2 layers are added with `+`.

---

## A First Scatterplot {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "GDP per capita vs. life expectancy, 2007"
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp,
    color = continent
  )) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (USD)",
    y = "Life Expectancy (years)",
    color = "Continent"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

The plot in @fig-scatter shows the classic wealth-health relationship: richer countries tend to have longer life expectancies.

Notice:

- data piped into `ggplot()`
- `aes()` maps variables to visual properties
- `geom_point()` adds the points layer
- `theme_bw()` gives a clean look
- `labs()` adds axis labels

:::
::::::

---

## Log Scale and `facet_wrap()` -- A Taste of What's Coming {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| label: fig-facet
#| fig-cap: "GDP vs. life expectancy by continent, 2007"
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp
  )) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  facet_wrap(~continent) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Life Expectancy"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

`scale_x_log10()` -- log-transforms the x axis, making the skewed GDP distribution easier to read.

`facet_wrap(~ continent)` -- creates one small panel per continent.

. . .

We will go much deeper on `ggplot2` next week. Today you are just seeing the shape of it.

:::
::::::

# Part VIII: Quarto Cell Options

---

## Execution Options -- Global vs. Cell Level {.smaller}

Last week you saw `echo` and `eval` as cell-level options. You can also set them **globally** in the YAML header so they apply to all cells at once:

```yaml
execute:
  eval: true    # run all code cells
  echo: true    # show all source code
  warning: false  # suppress warnings (e.g. tidyverse startup)
  message: false  # suppress messages
  error: false  # stop on errors (set true to render despite errors)
  freeze: true  # don't re-run cells unless source changes
```

. . .

Cell-level options **override** global ones for that specific cell:

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| echo: false   # overrides global echo: true for this cell only
my_plot
```
````

---

## Cell Options Reference {.smaller}

| Option | Values | What it controls |
|---|---|---|
| `eval` | `true` / `false` | Whether the code is executed |
| `echo` | `true` / `false` | Whether the source code is shown |
| `warning` | `true` / `false` | Whether warnings appear in output |
| `message` | `true` / `false` | Whether messages appear in output |
| `error` | `true` / `false` | Whether errors stop rendering |
| `include` | `true` / `false` | Whether cell output appears at all |
| `code-fold` | `true` / `false` / `"show"` | Collapsible code block |
| `label` | `fig-*` / `tbl-*` / any string | Cell identifier for cross-referencing |
| `fig-cap` | string | Caption for figure output |
| `fig-width` | number | Figure width in inches |
| `fig-height` | number | Figure height in inches |

---

## Labels and Cross-References {.smaller}

Labelling a code cell that produces a figure enables **automatic cross-references** in your prose:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: fig-lifeexp
#| fig-cap: "Life expectancy over time"
#| message: false

gapminder |>
  group_by(year, continent) |>
  summarise(mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_le,
             color = continent)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_bw()
```

As shown in @fig-lifeexp, life
expectancy has risen across all
continents since 1952.
````

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- The `label` must start with `fig-` for figures, `tbl-` for tables
- `@fig-lifeexp` in prose becomes "Figure 1" (auto-numbered) in the rendered output
- Figure numbers update automatically if you add or reorder figures
- Works in HTML, PDF, and Word output

. . .

This is how academic papers keep figure references consistent. Your future self will thank you.

:::
::::::

---

## Suppressing Tidyverse Messages {.smaller}

When you load `tidyverse`, R prints a message listing attached packages. When you use `read_csv()`, it prints column types. These are useful interactively, but clutter your rendered document.

. . .

Suppress them with cell options or globally:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Cell level:**

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
```
````

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Global YAML (applies to all cells):**

```yaml
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
```

:::
::::::

. . .

Prefer the global approach in homework submissions so your rendered PDF looks clean.

# Time to Practice!

---

## Walkthrough Overview

- Clone the Week 4 exercise repo from GitHub (same workflow as previous weeks)
- Open `exercise_week04.qmd` in Positron
- Work through the tasks in order using the **gapminder** dataset
- **Fast finishers:** optional depth exercises at the bottom of the document

::: notes
Live-load gapminder, show glimpse(), run a filter-mutate-summarise pipe together, then let them work. Circulate. Reserve ~10 min for questions and homework preview.
:::

---

## Homework

- Distributed via GitHub (same workflow as previous weeks)
- Submit a rendered Quarto document (`.qmd` + rendered PDF)
- **Due:** one week from today

The homework follows the arc of today's session. You will work with the gapminder dataset to practice `dplyr` wrangling and produce at least one properly labelled and captioned figure that you cross-reference in your prose. The rendered document must use `message: false` and `warning: false` globally so that the PDF is clean.

# Thank you for your attention and see you next week!

<a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a>
